cards.front: What signals in LC 217 — Contains Duplicate tell you to reach for a HashSet rather than sorting or brute force?
cards.answer:
Three signals together point to HashSet:
1. The question is pure membership — 'have I seen this value before?' — not counting, not ordering.
2. No O(1) space constraint is given, so you are free to use auxiliary memory.
3. n up to 10^5 rules out O(n^2) brute force, and O(n) is achievable with a set, so there is no reason to pay the O(n log n) cost of sorting.
cards.note: The unlock: any time the core question is 'have I seen X before?', that is the canonical HashSet use case. A set converts that question from O(n) linear scan to O(1) average lookup.
cards.application: LC 1 (Two Sum), LC 128 (Longest Consecutive Sequence), LC 349 (Intersection of Two Arrays) — all ask 'have I seen this before?' inside a loop.

cards.front: How does the HashSet solution to LC 217 — Contains Duplicate work?
cards.answer:
Maintain a set of values seen so far as you scan left to right.
Before inserting each element, check if it already exists in the set.
If it does, a duplicate has been found — return True immediately.
If the loop finishes with no hit, return False.
The set enables O(1) average lookup so the check costs nothing per element.
cards.note: The check must come BEFORE the insert. If you insert first, you will always find the element you just added.

cards.front: What invariant must hold at every step of the HashSet solution to LC 217?
cards.answer:
At the start of each iteration i, the set `seen` contains exactly the elements at indices 0 through i-1 — every element processed so far, and nothing else.
This guarantees that checking `num in seen` before insertion is equivalent to asking: 'does this value appear anywhere earlier in the array?'
cards.note: If you ever add to the set after the check in the same iteration, the invariant still holds at the start of the next iteration. The order within one iteration is: check → insert → advance.

cards.front: Trace the LC 217 HashSet algorithm on nums = [4, 9, 4, 2].
cards.answer:
Input: nums = [4, 9, 4, 2]

Step | Value | In seen? | seen after
-----|-------|----------|------------
  1  |   4   |    No    | {4}
  2  |   9   |    No    | {4, 9}
  3  |   4   |   YES    | → return True

Result: True (4 appears at index 0 and index 2)
cards.note: The algorithm exits at step 3 without ever reading index 3. Early exit is the key practical advantage over the one-liner len(set(nums)) != len(nums), which always reads every element.

cards.front: What is the time and space complexity of the optimal HashSet solution to LC 217, and why does each bound arise?
cards.answer:
Time: O(n) — the loop runs exactly n iterations. Each `in` check and `add` on a Python set is O(1) average (hash table). So total work is n x O(1) = O(n).
Space: O(n) — in the worst case (all unique values) the set grows to hold all n elements before the loop ends.
cards.note: The sort-based approach is O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space. Choose HashSet when space is not constrained and you want the fastest possible time. Choose sort when you cannot use extra memory.

cards.front: What is the most dangerous implementation trap in LC 217 when using a HashSet, and what silent bug does it produce?
cards.answer:
Inserting into the set BEFORE checking membership.
If you write `seen.add(num)` then `if num in seen`, the check always returns True because you just added the element — every element looks like a duplicate of itself.
The fix: always check membership first, then insert only if not found.
cards.note: This bug passes the logic check in your head ('I add it, then I check') but produces wrong output every time. The correct mental model is a doorman: check the guest list before adding the new arrival, not after.

cards.front: Why is brute force O(n^2) insufficient for LC 217, and what is the key insight that enables O(n)?
cards.answer:
Brute force compares every pair (i, j) — for each element it scans all remaining elements to find a match. With n up to 10^5, that is 10^10 comparisons — two orders of magnitude over the 10^8 budget.
The insight: instead of asking 'does this element match anything after it?' (requires scanning forward), ask 'does this element match anything before it?' A HashSet stores everything seen so far and answers that question in O(1). The direction flip converts O(n) scans into O(1) lookups.
cards.note: This direction flip — from looking forward to looking backward using stored history — is the core idea behind almost every HashSet speedup. The set IS the history.

cards.front: Write the optimal HashSet solution for LC 217 — Contains Duplicate.
cards.answer: Loop through nums, check before insert, return True on first hit, return False if loop completes.
cards.code:
def containsDuplicate(nums: list[int]) -> bool:
    seen = set()
    for num in nums:
        if num in seen:      # O(1) average — duplicate found
            return True
        seen.add(num)        # O(1) average — mark as visited
    return False             # no duplicate found after full scan

cards.front: Name 2-3 LeetCode problems that share the same core pattern as LC 217 — Contains Duplicate, and state the shared pattern.
cards.answer:
Shared pattern: 'have I seen this value before?' — use a HashSet to answer in O(1) while scanning.
1. LC 1 — Two Sum: for each num, check if (target - num) is already in a seen map.
2. LC 128 — Longest Consecutive Sequence: put all values in a set, then for each sequence start check how far it extends using set lookups.
3. LC 349 — Intersection of Two Arrays: build a set from array A, scan array B checking membership.
cards.note: The pattern is always: build a set from what you have processed, then use O(1) lookup to answer a historical question about future elements.

cards.front: What is the difference between `return len(set(nums)) != len(nums)` and the loop-based HashSet solution for LC 217?
cards.answer:
Both are O(n) time and O(n) space and both produce correct output.
The key difference is early exit: the loop returns True the moment it finds a duplicate without processing the rest of the array. The one-liner always builds the complete set regardless of where the duplicate sits.
If the duplicate is at index 1 in a 10^5 element array, the loop does 2 iterations; the one-liner does 10^5.
cards.note: In interviews, show the loop version first to demonstrate you understand the algorithm, then offer the one-liner as a Pythonic alternative. Never show only the one-liner — it looks like you googled the answer.

cards.front: What signals in LC 1929 — Concatenation of Array tell you this is a pure array construction problem requiring no clever data structure?
cards.answer:
Three signals confirm this is straightforward construction:
1. The output length is exactly 2n — a fixed, known size determined before any computation.
2. The problem statement gives you the exact output formula: ans[i] == ans[i+n] == nums[i].
3. No space constraint, no sorted requirement, no membership check — nothing that calls for a hash structure or two pointers.
When the problem hands you the index formula, implement it directly.
cards.note: The danger here is overthinking. If you find yourself reaching for a sliding window or hash map, stop. The problem is testing whether you can read an index relationship and express it cleanly.
cards.application: LC 189 (Rotate Array), LC 1752 (Check if Array is Sorted and Rotated), LC 622 (Design Circular Queue) — all use modulo to map a linear index into a circular range.

cards.front: How does the modulo index solution to LC 1929 — Concatenation of Array work?
cards.answer:
Allocate a result array of size 2n.
For each index i from 0 to 2n-1, set result[i] = nums[i % n].
The modulo operation maps i into the range [0, n-1]: for the first half (i < n) it returns i unchanged; for the second half (i >= n) it wraps back to i - n, which is exactly the corresponding position in nums.
cards.note: i % n is the key. It converts a linear index in a 2n-length array into a circular index in an n-length array. This same trick appears in every cyclic/repeat pattern.

cards.front: What invariant must hold at every step of the LC 1929 construction loop?
cards.answer:
At the start of iteration i, all positions 0 through i-1 in the result array have been correctly filled with nums[j % n] for their respective j.
This means the first copy of nums is complete once i reaches n, and the second copy mirrors it exactly for indices n through 2n-1.
cards.note: Because each position is written exactly once and read from a stable source (nums), there is no risk of overwriting or reading stale data. The invariant is trivially maintained.

cards.front: Trace the LC 1929 modulo index algorithm on nums = [7, 2, 5].
cards.note: Notice i=3,4,5 produce the same modulo values as i=0,1,2. The second half is mechanically identical to the first half — modulo enforces the repeat.

cards.front: What is the time and space complexity of the optimal LC 1929 solution, and why?
cards.answer:
Time: O(n) — the loop runs 2n iterations, each doing one modulo operation and one array write, both O(1). Total = O(2n) = O(n).
Space: O(n) — the result array of size 2n is the only allocation. No auxiliary structures are created.
cards.note: The in-place append alternative is O(1) extra space because it extends the input array rather than creating a new one — but it mutates the input, which must be flagged in an interview.

cards.front: What is the most dangerous implementation trap in LC 1929, specifically in the in-place append approach?
cards.answer:
Iterating over nums with `for x in nums` while appending to it causes an infinite loop.
Python's for-each loop re-evaluates len(nums) each iteration, so as you append elements the loop boundary keeps moving forward and the loop never terminates.
The fix: capture n = len(nums) before the loop and use `for i in range(n)` with `nums.append(nums[i])`.
cards.note: This is one of the rare cases where modifying a collection while iterating over it causes not just a wrong answer but a program hang. Always freeze the original length before mutation loops.

cards.front: Why is there no smarter-than-O(n) solution for LC 1929, and what does the problem actually test?
cards.answer:
You must write 2n elements into the output — that is a hard lower bound. No algorithm can fill 2n positions in fewer than 2n steps, so O(n) is optimal by definition.
The problem does not test algorithmic cleverness. It tests: can you read an index formula (ans[i] == nums[i % n]) and implement it cleanly without introducing bugs? It is a warm-up problem for index arithmetic and modulo thinking.
cards.note: When a problem gives you the output formula explicitly in the description, that is a strong signal to implement it directly rather than searching for a pattern. Not every problem has a trick.

cards.front: Write the optimal solution for LC 1929 — Concatenation of Array.
cards.answer: Python list concatenation is the cleanest expression of the problem statement.

cards.front: Name 2-3 LeetCode problems that share the modulo index pattern introduced in LC 1929, and state what they share.
cards.answer:
Shared pattern: i % n maps a linear index into a circular or repeated range of size n.
1. LC 189 — Rotate Array: the rotated position of element i is (i + k) % n.
2. LC 1752 — Check if Array is Sorted and Rotated: compare nums[i] with nums[(i+1) % n] to detect the wrap-around point.
3. LC 622 — Design Circular Queue: head and tail pointers advance as (ptr + 1) % capacity to wrap around the fixed-size buffer.
cards.note: Whenever you need an index to wrap back to the start after reaching the end, % n is the tool. It converts a linear counter into a circular one.
